metadata.title: Build Fermi's Golden Rule with Claude Code
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = rate curve (quadratic). CRIMSON = reference rate point. INK for all Text.

beats.narration_text: Medhavy A-I. From Sanskrit — meaning intelligent, or intellectually brilliant. The perfect name for our A-I-powered intelligent learning systems.

lines: — the perfect name for our AI-powered

beats.narration_text: The prompt. Claude Code terminal: claude — plot Fermi's Golden Rule transition rate as a function of coupling strength V. Rate equals two pi over h-bar times V-squared times the density of states rho. Use rho equals the hydrogen two-p to one-s value, one-point-six nanoseconds lifetime — that gives rho from the rate. Show V from zero to two electron-volts on the x-axis, rate in units of ten to the eighth per second on the y-axis. Mark the H two-p point. Specify: the quadratic scaling and the log-log slope of two.

props.command:
claude "plot Fermi Golden Rule rate vs coupling V:
  Γ = (2π/ℏ)|V|²ρ(E)
  V ∈ [0, 2] eV; ρ from H 2p→1s lifetime τ=1.6 ns
  y-axis: Γ in units of 10⁸ s⁻¹
  mark H 2p point; show quadratic scaling"

beats.narration_text: The CLI writes it. Read before running. Two checks. First: the prefactor. The rate must scale as V-squared. The Golden Rule formula is two pi over h-bar times the matrix element squared times the density of states. If the code squares a different quantity, the curve has the wrong power. Second: the reference point. At the hydrogen two-p matrix element — approximately four-point-two times ten to the minus five electron-volts — the rate should equal six-point-three times ten to the eighth per second. That is the NIST Lyman-alpha A-coefficient. Thirty seconds.

props.code:
# golden_rule.py  —  Claude Code output
import numpy as np

hbar_eVs = 6.582e-16  # eV·s

# H 2p→1s: A = 6.27e8 s⁻¹ (NIST Lyman-alpha)
A_H = 6.27e8

# density of states from lifetime: ρ = Γ/(2π/ℏ)|V|²
# Use V_H ~ 4.2e-5 eV (Lyman-alpha matrix element)
V_H = 4.2e-5   # eV
rho = A_H / ((2*np.pi/hbar_eVs) * V_H**2)

# Rate vs coupling: Γ(V) = (2π/ℏ) ρ V²
V  = np.linspace(0, 2, 400)
Gamma = (2*np.pi/hbar_eVs) * rho * V**2

beats.narration_text: Run it. Two checks. First: the curve shape. The teal parabola opens upward. A straight line on a log-log plot has slope two — rate doubles when V increases by root two, quadruples when V doubles. Second: the crimson reference dot. At the hydrogen matrix element, the rate reads six-point-three times ten to the eighth per second. That is the A-coefficient. It is the same number Dirac derived in nineteen twenty-seven. Doubling the coupling means the rate goes up by a factor of four. The periodic table of emission spectra is built on that quadratic.

Iterate: claude — update golden underscore rule dot py — add a dashed line showing the linear scaling that the rate would have if it went as V instead of V-squared. One diff, one re-run. Prompt, read, run, check, change. The rate curve was the vehicle. The loop is the takeaway.

props.command:
claude "update golden_rule.py:
  → add dashed Γ ∝ V line (linear, not quadratic)
  → label both curves"

beats.narration_text: Medhavy. A-I-powered intelligent learning systems. On YouTube, at Medhavy A-I. Medhavy dot com.
